title: Fertilizer guidelines — principles
prose: A fertilizer guideline is co-ordinated information that has been compiled in such a way that it can be used with relative ease to rationalise decision-making regarding fertilizer input and timing for a specific crop to ensure profitable production. PRINCIPLES A fertilizer guideline would ideally be based on properly controlled research where crop reaction (mostly yield and quality) is correlated with soil and/or plant analyses (leaf and sap), climatic and soil or site parameters. This data would be integrated in crop production functions in accordance with accepted scientific techniques. A high measure of scientific expertise is required for these techniques, as specified in Act 36 of 1947. Although scientific fertilizer guidelines are available for a number of economic important crops (such as maize, wheat and sugar cane), relevant research results for many crops are lacking to a greater or lesser extent. In such cases, fertilizer guidelines are based on research data that is available and supplemented by information from one or more of the following sources: collected data from record-keeping, whether by the farmer or a study group; estimated removal of plant nutrients by the crop; extrapolation of knowledge gained with other crops (for example the probability of a yield reaction to applied P or K on soils showing a deficiency of these elements); international data; and relevant knowledge and experience of the adviser and the farmer. See also Chapter 1.5, introductory paragraphs. NEW TECHNOLOGY A guideline can never make provision for all variables. As new technology in crop production is applied, guidelines will have to be adjusted to accommodate the effect of new variables. Therefore, calibration research referred to above can never be considered to be finalised. GUIDELINES IN THIS MANUAL For the reasons stated above, the guidelines in this manual are also not the last word on this subject, and should be regarded as a general point of departure. Specialist advisers should be consulted for adjustments according to local conditions. This may, for instance, include adjustments with regard to nitrogen requirements of different cultivars, the effect of varying rainfall following planting or sowing, quality requirements, smart sampling and precision farming.
